ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho. FOODON_00002700 (beef noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class covering braised beef with noodles in broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Beef soup with noodles) is the closest available parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese-style braised beef stew with thin rice noodles (hủ tiếu bò kho).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish. SNOMED CT does not have granular coverage of Southeast Asian prepared dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of thin rice noodles topped with braised beef pieces and vegetables, served with a rich spiced broth.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup / Vietnamese-style braised beef noodle dish — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (beef brisket braised, rice noodles cooked, broth) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 30% braised beef brisket (USDA FDC #174031), 35% cooked thin rice noodles (USDA FDC #169730), 30% beef broth with vegetables (USDA FDC #172956), and 5% aromatics/seasonings. Values are cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese beef noodle dishes. Per-100g values reflect the composite dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and beef stew analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being thin rice noodles. The University of Sydney GI Database reports rice noodles (fresh/cooked) at GI 40–61 (mean ~53). A composite GI of 55 is applied, accounting for the moderating effect of protein (beef) and fat on glycemic response. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (55 × 51.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 28.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, beef broth, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving of Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho provides approximately 1,890 mg sodium, representing 82–94% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index rice noodles with moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 28.5 per serving, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, choose lean beef cuts, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from broth and fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 5 g saturated fat and 1,890 mg sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, request lean beef, and avoid drinking the full broth to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho (Beef Stew with Thin Rice Noodles) is a Vietnamese noodle dish consisting of slow-braised beef — typically brisket or shank — served over thin, silky rice noodles (hủ tiếu) in a richly spiced, aromatic broth. The stew is flavoured with lemongrass, star anise, cinnamon, annatto (for its characteristic deep orange-red colour), garlic, shallots, and fish sauce, producing a complex, savoury, and mildly sweet flavour profile. The beef is braised until tender and yielding, while the thin rice noodles provide a soft, delicate texture that absorbs the broth. Vegetables such as carrots and daikon radish are commonly added. The dish is served hot, often garnished with fresh herbs, bean sprouts, and a wedge of lime. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g restaurant serving provides approximately 441 kcal, 32 g protein, 14 g fat, and 52 g carbohydrate, with a high sodium content (~1,890 mg) due to the broth and fish sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), making it a moderate glycemic load meal. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and therefore of concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease; the moderate GI and carbohydrate content require portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is popular in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia, and is distinct from the southern Vietnamese hủ tiếu Nam Vang (Phnom Penh-style) by its use of braised beef stew rather than clear pork broth.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for braised beef brisket, cooked thin rice noodles, and beef broth, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese noodle dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate analogue. Confidence is medium due to the estimation methodology and restaurant-level variability in recipe composition.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the approved source data for this estimated composite dish. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant in Singapore (GrabFood listing) at SGD 15.90. The cuisine is classified as Vietnamese (not Singaporean) as the dish originates from Vietnam; however, multilingual naming follows the Singaporean context rules given the serving location. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains beef from a Vietnamese restaurant context where halal certification is not confirmed. Gluten-free status is false due to possible cross-contamination and some recipe variants using wheat-based noodles. The food_group is retained as 'Meat and alternatives' per the input record, which is defensible given beef is the primary protein component.
